title: Ketamine induces multiple individually distinct whole-brain functional connectivity signatures
abstract_summary: Acute ketamine administration in 40 healthy participants produces a multidimensional pattern of whole-brain functional connectivity changes. A data-driven PCA of delta global brain connectivity (GBC) maps reveals five significant principal components capturing 42.1% of variance; the two dominant components separate association from sensory networks and map onto somatostatin (SST) and parvalbumin (PVALB) cortical gene expression. A parallel PCA on 31 behavioral measures (PANSS and cognition) reveals two axes (PC1 explains 29%, PC2 explains 12% of variance). Neuro-behavioral PC1 is highly correlated across individuals with behavioral PC1 (r=0.7) and with PANSS Positive (r=0.69), Negative (r=0.93), and General (r=0.89) maps, supporting individually precise pharmacological biomarkers.

sections.name: Introduction
goals: Motivate ketamine as a rapid-acting antidepressant (TRD response rate ~65%).
Highlight gaps: inter-individual variability and limited whole-brain mechanistic mapping.
State aim: data-driven decomposition of neural + behavioral ketamine effects and mapping to cortical SST/PVALB gene expression.

sections.name: Results
goals: R1: PCA on delta GBC reveals five significant PCs (42.1% variance); PC1=14.1%, PC2=10.4%.
R2: Effective dimensionality: ketamine (12.8 +/- 0.7) > LSD (8.7 +/- 0.3) and psilocybin (8.6 +/- 0.3); F(2,60)=564, p<.001.
R3: PC1 DeltaGBC tracks SST (r=0.47, p<.001) and PVALB (r=-0.22, p=.025).
R4: Ketamine impairs cognition and increases PANSS positive/negative/general symptoms.
R5: Behavioral PCA yields 2 significant PCs (41.1% variance; PC1=29%, PC2=12%); Pitman-Morgan t=6.4, df=38, p<.001.
R6: Neuro-behavioral PC1 separates association vs sensory networks and tracks SST (r=0.29, p=.009) and PVALB (r=-0.14, p=.089).
R7: Behavioral PC1 vs neuro-behavioral PC1 across subjects: r=0.7, p<.001.
R8: Neuro-behavioral PC1 vs PANSS maps: Positive r=0.69, Negative r=0.93, General r=0.89; all p<.001.
R9: Single-subject resolvability: subject 22 delta GBC vs SST r=-0.33, p<.001; subject 19 delta GBC vs PVALB r=-0.17, p=.027.

sections.name: Discussion
goals: Paragraph 1: Context - summarize that ketamine's neural fingerprint is multidimensional and anchored on SST/PVALB microcircuits.
Paragraph 2: Relation to prior GBC and pharmaco-fMRI literature showing robust brain-wide ketamine effects.
Paragraph 3: Comparison with classical psychedelics (LSD, psilocybin) - ketamine is higher in dimensionality.
Paragraph 4: Clinical implications for TRD biomarker stratification and precision psychiatry.
Paragraph 5: Molecular anchor - SST/PVALB interneuron hypothesis for ketamine's antidepressant and psychotomimetic effects.
Paragraph 6: Limitations (double-blind failure due to subjective effects, N=40 healthy cohort, acute administration only) and future directions.

sections.name: Methods
goals: Describe double-blind placebo-controlled design with acute ketamine (initial bolus 0.23 mg/kg, continuous infusion 0.58 mg/kg/hour) in N=40 healthy participants.
Describe GBC pipeline: 718 functionally-defined parcels, Delta GBC maps, 718 x 40 PCA input matrix.
Describe permutation tests (5000 permutations for PC significance; 100,000 permutations for spatial correlations with gene expression).
Describe behavioral measures (PANSS subscales + cognition) and PCA on 31 behavioral items.
Describe comparison datasets for LSD and psilocybin dimensionality.
